results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg (died 2018), who is likely the artist referred to as D. Rathenberg, was a mathematician, composer, and theorist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a pioneer in the intersection of art, music, and technology and was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, specifically the E.A.T. Los Angeles survey. In the mid-1960s, he collaborated with synthesizer pioneer Robert Moog to design and build a unique analog synthesizer and 31-tone octave keyboard, an instrument funded in part by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research. His work focused on pattern recognition and the perception of pitch, which he applied to models for inter-species communication (including potential communication with 'alien species' through music). He published several technical reports and papers on these subjects, including 'A Pattern-Recognition Model Applied to the Perception of Pitch' (1969) and 'An Adaptive Pattern Representation and Recognition System' (1970). In later years, he was involved in formal methods for music composition and computer-generated film, contributing to seminars at institutions like Mills College. His name is frequently misspelled as 'Rathenberg' in academic citations and book references (notably in citations for the 1995 book 'Wild Ideas').

David Rothenberg (born 1962) is a contemporary artist, jazz clarinetist, and philosopher who is frequently cited as 'D. Rathenberg' in academic and literary contexts, particularly in references to the book 'Wild Ideas: Toward Something Else' (1995). While he was a child during the late 1960s and early 1970s, his work as a 'interspecies musician'—performing and recording music with birds, whales, and insects—shares the technological and scientific collaborative spirit of E.A.T. He is currently a Distinguished Professor of Philosophy and Music at the New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT), where he works with engineers and scientists. Due to the frequent misspelling of his surname as 'Rathenberg' in citations, he may be the individual identified by that name in later bibliographies related to art and ecology.

results.possibleIdentity: Norma-Jean Squires (also referred to as Norma Jean Squires) is a 20th-century artist and educator primarily active in Southern California, New Jersey, and Ontario. She graduated from The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art in 1961. Her artistic career spans several decades, with significant activity beginning in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is known for her abstract works in painting and sculpture, often utilizing mediums such as acrylic, interference colors, prismacolor, oilstick, and pastel. Squires was featured in the 1975 exhibition '4x8+4x4' at the Newport Harbor Art Museum, which showcased young Southern California artists. Her later work includes the exhibition 'Passageways' (1994) and participation in the 'Lantern of the East' International Art Festival (2002). In addition to her studio practice, she has been an art critic and a member of the Southern California Women's Caucus for Art (SCWCA). She has lived and worked in Playa del Rey and North Hills, California.

results.possibleIdentity: Aleksandra Kasuba (1923–2019), born Aleksandra Fledžinskaitė-Kašubienė, was a Lithuanian-American environmental artist and visionary designer who was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is widely recognized for her pioneering work in 'space shelters' and immersive environmental structures created from tensile fabrics (such as nylon) that emphasized organic shapes and the absence of right angles. Kasuba was a notable participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, notably contributing to the landmark 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum with her plexiglass structure 'Gateway'. Her work during the early 1970s included the social utopia project 'Global Village' (1971–1972) and her manifesto 'Utility for the Soul' (1970). Beyond her fabric environments, she designed significant public architectural works, including a 4,000-square-foot etched granite wall at the World Trade Center (destroyed in 2001) and various brick murals for institutions like the Rochester Institute of Technology. She lived and worked primarily in New York City before moving to New Mexico in her later years.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Sterling Neuman (1926–2015) was an American abstract painter, printmaker, and art educator who was prominent in the art scenes of both the San Francisco Bay Area and the East Coast. He was particularly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, serving as a professor at Harvard University's Carpenter Center for Visual Arts from 1965 to 1972 and later as the chair of the art department at Keene State College. Neuman's work is characterized by its evolution from Abstract Expressionism into geometric abstraction and symbolism, often organized into thematic series such as "Pedazos del Mundo," "Space Signs," and "Ship to Paradise." His potential association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is suggested by his early interest in the intersection of art and science; notably, his 1952 work "Conscienceless Machine" was recognized for representing a "union of science, technology, and art." Furthermore, his professional papers and records are archived at the Smithsonian Institution and are frequently cited alongside those of major figures in the art-and-technology movement, such as Otto Piene.

results.possibleIdentity: Arthur William "Bill" Brody (born March 2, 1943) is an American artist, printmaker, and educator known for his long-standing career at the University of Alaska Fairbanks (UAF), where he is a Professor Emeritus. Brody’s work is characterized by a unique fusion of traditional fine arts and high technology. He holds a Bachelor of Science in Mathematics from Harvey Mudd College (1965) and an MFA in Painting and Printmaking from Claremont Graduate School (1967). He began his teaching career at UAF in 1967 and was active through the late 1960s and 1970s, teaching at Ripon College from 1970 to 1975 before returning to Alaska. In the late 1960s, he was involved in theoretical work concerning interactive environments and cybernetics; a 1967 article by him proposed seeing surroundings as "self-organizing intelligence," a concept that aligned with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the work of contemporaries like Nicholas Negroponte. Later in his career, Brody served as a research specialist at UAF’s Arctic Region Supercomputing Center, where he explored the intersection of art and virtual reality. He is well-regarded for developing the Body Language User Interface (BLUI), a system for 3D gestural drawing in immersive environments. His traditional work often focuses on large-scale landscape paintings of the Alaskan wilderness, woodcuts, and carved metal panels.

results.possibleIdentity: Joel Heathcote (died September 9, 2024) was a self-described "Artrepreneur" and lighting artist known as "Joel, the Light Man." A 1961 graduate of Dartmouth College, his artistic career began in earnest in 1968 when he left the corporate world and moved to New York City. There, he worked on large-scale rock and roll light shows at the famous Fillmore East, an experience that influenced his lifelong focus on the intersection of light, technology, and art. In the 1970s, he was active in Southern California, where he founded an artist collective called WonderWorks and established an art gallery in Laguna Beach. His work spanned several decades and included the creation of laser light shows, fiber optic "Night Sky" ceilings, and color therapy devices such as the "Zen Light" and "Body Light." Heathcote was also involved in multi-media and environmental design projects, including participation in events related to Buckminster Fuller's "The World Game" at San Diego State University. In his later years, he designed a sustainable rustic habitat known as "Forest Moon" in New Hampshire.

segment.text: There, he worked on large-scale rock and roll light shows at the famous Fillmore East, an experience that influenced his lifelong focus on the intersection of light, technology, and art.

results.possibleIdentity: Ira Schneider (1939–2022) was a pioneering American video artist and a central figure in the early video art movement of the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his seminal 1969 multi-channel video installation "Wipe Cycle," co-created with Frank Gillette and exhibited at the landmark "TV as a Creative Medium" show at the Howard Wise Gallery in New York. Schneider was a founding member of the Raindance Foundation (formerly the Raindance Corporation), a video collective and media think tank established in 1969, and he served as its president from 1972 to 1994. He also co-founded and edited the influential video journal "Radical Software" (1970) and co-edited the first comprehensive book on the medium, "Video Art: An Anthology" (1976), with Beryl Korot. While historical records of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) frequently mention a "Schneider" in the context of the 1966 "9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering," this refers to the Bell Labs engineer Herb Schneider; however, Ira Schneider was a contemporary in the same New York scene, and his work was often featured in exhibitions alongside E.A.T.-associated artists. Schneider studied experimental psychology at Brown University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison before turning to film and then video. He lived and worked in Berlin from 1993 until his return to New York in 2021, shortly before his death in 2022.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Lovenberg is an artist and videomaker active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly within the experimental and electronic art scene in New York City. He is closely associated with the early video art community, including Steina and Woody Vasulka, the founders of The Kitchen. Lovenberg participated in significant cross-disciplinary events such as the 1970 Earth Day celebration in Union Square, where he presented works titled 'Experiments' (with Bellinger) and 'Electronic Interferences #2.' His work often involved electronic imaging and audio-visual collaborations, including projects at WBAI with figures like Archie Shepp and Joe Lee ('Tissues') and the Bartok Trio ('Energies'). Lovenberg's practice aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as his projects often bridged the gap between artistic expression and technological experimentation. He remained engaged with the field into the late 1990s, maintaining an interest in concepts such as 'Ambient Intelligence.'

results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth (Ken) Marsh is a video artist, painter, and graphics designer active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a pioneer of the early video art movement and community video activism. In the late 1960s, he initially worked with light shows before co-founding the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City around 1969-1970 with Elliot Glass and Howard Gutstadt. The group utilized the newly available Portapak cameras to document community issues, street protests, and social movements (such as the Young Lords and Black Panthers), providing an alternative to mainstream network television. Marsh was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that fostered collaborations between the arts and engineering sectors, and he is cited as a member in records detailing the organization's history. He is also the author of the influential technical manual 'Independent Video' (1974), which served as a guide for artists and activists using television technology. His work often focused on 'activating the information flow' and using video as a tool for participatory democracy.

results.possibleIdentity: Arthur W. Segil (Arthur William Segil, 1930–2016) was a lighting and environmental systems specialist whose work in the late 1960s and 1970s bridged the gap between engineering and the arts. He is most recognized as the co-author, with John Edward Flynn, of the foundational book "Architectural Interior Systems: Lighting, Air Conditioning, Acoustics" (first published in 1970 by Van Nostrand Reinhold). The book was pioneering for its focus on how technical systems like lighting and acoustics could be designed to meet human sensory and behavioral needs, a philosophy that aligned with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. In the context of E.A.T., technical experts like Segil often collaborated with artists to create immersive environments and installations. Based in Chicago, Segil was the CEO of the Integra-Lite Company and a prominent figure in the city's cultural life, serving as a long-time supporter of the Ravinia Festival and a dedicated art collector who acquired works by significant artists of the era, such as the kinetic and Op Art pioneer Carlos Cruz-Diez. His background in a family with strong ties to the arts—including his father Leon Segil and aunt Sonia Sharnova—further positioned him at the intersection of technical innovation and artistic practice.
